topic: FFI - Thread Safety Contracts Across FFI

turns.content: Hey, I'm reading the Rust FFI documentation and it's really confusing. Can someone explain how thread safety works when calling C libraries from Rust?

Absolutely! When you call into a C library from Rust via FFI, one of the first things to consider is whether that C code is thread-safe or not. Many C libraries are not designed with concurrent use in mind, so simply calling them from multiple threads can lead to undefined behavior.
That makes sense. So, what should I do if I want to call a non-thread-safe C library from multiple Rust threads?

Good question! The safest way is to ensure that each thread has its own isolated instance of the C library or uses synchronization primitives to coordinate access. One common approach is wrapping your FFI calls in a Mutex, so only one thread can execute at a time.
Oh, I see. And what about unsafe types? Like if I have an FFI handle that implements Send, does that mean it's safe to share between threads?

No, sharing an FFI handle that implements Send isn't necessarily safe. The C library might not be thread-safe itself, and Rust's guarantees around Send don't magically make unsafe code thread-safe. You'd need to ensure the C calls are protected by a Mutex or similar mechanism when accessed concurrently.
Right, so if I have FFI handles that can only be used on a single thread—like GTK widgets—I should never share them across threads?

Exactly. One common pattern is to use thread-local storage (TLS) in Rust or the C library itself to store per-thread state, so each thread can have its own instance of the handle without worrying about concurrency issues. Alternatively, you can expose a function that initializes handles on-demand, tied to the current OS thread.
Ah, I think I get it. So, if a C library isn't explicitly marked as thread-safe, I should always wrap FFI calls in Mutexes and avoid sharing unsafe handles between threads?

Yes! That's the safest practice. But be cautious with crates like GTK or other GUI toolkits that often require thread-local state. You may need to design your Rust code around initializing such libraries per-thread and avoiding cross-thread sharing. This way, you avoid subtle concurrency bugs and keep things predictable.
